title: Python for Absolute Beginners: From Zero to Writing Real Programs
subtitle: A complete beginner's journey through Python — no prior coding experience needed
description: Learn to think like a programmer and write Python code that actually does useful things. This course takes you from installing Python on your computer all the way to building a working project you can show off, using plain English explanations at every step.
learningOutcomes: By the end of this course, students will be able to write Python programs that use variables, loops, conditionals, and functions to solve real problems.
By the end of this course, students will be able to read and debug Python error messages without panic.
By the end of this course, students will be able to work with lists, dictionaries, and files to store and process data.
By the end of this course, students will be able to design and build a small complete Python project from scratch.

modules.title: Your First Lines of Code: Python Basics
modules.description: Set up your Python environment and write your first programs. You'll learn what variables, data types, and print statements are, and why Python is one of the friendliest languages to start with.

lessons.title: Installing Python and Writing Hello World
lessons.objective: Students will be able to install Python, open a terminal, and run their first Python script.

lessons.title: Variables and Data Types
lessons.objective: Students will be able to create variables and explain the difference between strings, integers, floats, and booleans.

lessons.title: Getting Input and Doing Maths
lessons.objective: Students will be able to use input() to receive user input and perform arithmetic operations in Python.

quizQuestions.question: What will this code print?  x = 5  y = '5'  print(x + int(y))
quizQuestions.explanation: int(y) converts the string '5' to the integer 5. Adding two integers (5 + 5) gives 10. If you tried x + y without int() you'd get a TypeError because you can't add an integer and a string directly.

quizQuestions.question: A student writes: name = input('Enter your name: ')  print('Hello ' + name). What type is the variable 'name'?
quizQuestions.explanation: The input() function always returns a string, regardless of what the user types. If you need a number from input(), you must explicitly convert it with int() or float().

modules.title: Making Decisions: Conditionals and Loops
modules.description: This is where your programs start thinking for themselves. Learn how to use if/elif/else to make decisions based on data, and for/while loops to repeat actions automatically — the two most powerful ideas in all of programming.

lessons.title: If, Elif, Else: Teaching Python to Decide
lessons.objective: Students will be able to write conditional statements that execute different code blocks based on data values.

lessons.title: For Loops: Doing Things Repeatedly
lessons.objective: Students will be able to use for loops to iterate over a range of numbers and a list of items.

lessons.title: While Loops and Breaking Out
lessons.objective: Students will be able to write while loops that run until a condition is met and use break to exit early.

quizQuestions.question: What will this loop print?  for i in range(1, 5):  print(i)
quizQuestions.explanation: range(1, 5) generates numbers starting at 1 and stopping BEFORE 5. So it produces 1, 2, 3, 4. The stop value is always exclusive in Python's range().

quizQuestions.question: When should you use a while loop instead of a for loop?
options: When you know exactly how many times to repeat
When you need to repeat until a condition becomes false, but don't know how many iterations it will take
When you want to loop over items in a list
While loops are just slower for loops — never use them
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: When you need to repeat until a condition becomes false, but don't know how many iterations it will take
quizQuestions.explanation: For loops are ideal when you know the count upfront (e.g., 'do this 10 times'). While loops are better when the stopping condition depends on user input or data that changes during execution.

modules.title: Functions and Reusable Code
modules.description: Stop writing the same code twice. Functions are the fundamental tool for organising, reusing, and testing code. You'll learn to write functions that take inputs, do work, and return results.

lessons.title: Defining and Calling Functions
lessons.objective: Students will be able to define functions with def, call them, and explain why functions make code easier to maintain.

lessons.title: Scope and Default Parameters
lessons.objective: Students will be able to explain variable scope and write functions with optional default parameter values.

quizQuestions.question: What is wrong with this function?  def add(a, b):  total = a + b  print(total)  result = add(3, 4)  print(result)
options: The function uses print instead of return, so result will be None
You can't add numbers inside a function
The parameters a and b are invalid names
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: The function uses print instead of return, so result will be None
quizQuestions.explanation: print() displays a value but doesn't give it back to the caller. Without a return statement, Python functions implicitly return None. Using return total would allow result to hold the value 7.

quizQuestions.question: Which of these correctly defines a function with a default parameter?
quizQuestions.explanation: Default parameters use a single equals sign (=) with no spaces required, placed after the parameter name. Parameters with defaults must come after parameters without defaults.

modules.title: Data Structures: Lists and Dictionaries
modules.description: Real programs need to store and organise data. Lists and dictionaries are Python's two most-used data structures, and mastering them will let you build almost anything.

lessons.title: Lists: Ordered Collections
lessons.objective: Students will be able to create, modify, and iterate over Python lists using common list methods.

lessons.title: Dictionaries: Key-Value Storage
lessons.objective: Students will be able to create dictionaries, access values by key, and loop over dictionary items.

quizQuestions.question: What does this code produce?  scores = {'Alice': 95, 'Bob': 88}  print(scores.get('Charlie', 0))
quizQuestions.explanation: dict.get(key, default) returns the value if the key exists, or the default value if it doesn't. Since 'Charlie' is not in the dictionary, it returns 0 instead of raising a KeyError.

quizQuestions.question: Which is the correct way to add a new key 'city' with value 'Milan' to a dictionary named 'person'?
quizQuestions.explanation: Dictionaries use bracket notation for both reading and writing values. Assigning to a key that doesn't exist creates it automatically.

bonusResources.title: Python.org Official Tutorial
bonusResources.description: The official Python documentation tutorial is surprisingly readable and is the most authoritative reference for any language feature.

bonusResources.title: Automate the Boring Stuff with Python by Al Sweigart
bonusResources.description: A free, practical book full of real projects like automating spreadsheets, PDFs, and web scraping — perfect for applying what you've learned.

bonusResources.title: Replit.com
bonusResources.description: Free browser-based Python IDE — write and run Python without installing anything, great for practicing on the go.
